notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a solid order to review your employer benefits, even though June 1–5 shows no transactions yet (that’s likely just a timing/sync issue):

1) Health insurance + HSA / Health FSA
Start here because one medical event can dwarf most other expenses.

Action:

List what you usually spend in a year on copays, prescriptions, therapy, dental/vision, glasses/contacts.
If you’re on a high‑deductible health plan (HDHP), check if an HSA is available and whether you’re contributing.
If you’re not on an HDHP, check if you have a health FSA and whether your election roughly matches your predictable out‑of‑pocket costs.

2) Dependent Care FSA (if you have kids or other dependents needing care)
Next, because childcare/eldercare is often a huge, predictable line item.

Action:

Estimate annual daycare, preschool, after‑school, camps, nanny/babysitter for work hours, or adult day care.
If that’s in the $2,000–$5,000+/year range, see if a Dependent Care FSA is offered and how much you’re electing.

3) Retirement plan + employer match (401(k)/403(b), 457, 403(b), etc.)
This is effectively part of your compensation.

Action:

Check if there’s a match (e.g., 50% of the first 6% you contribute).
Make sure you’re contributing at least enough to get the full match, if cash flow allows.

4) Commuter benefits (if you commute regularly)
Good next step if you pay for transit or parking.

Action:

Estimate your typical monthly transit passes, parking, or vanpool costs.
If your employer offers pre‑tax transit/parking, compare your usual spend to the benefit limits and see if you’re enrolled.

5) Life and disability insurance through work
Important if your income supports others.

Action:

Check basic life insurance (often 1x salary) and any supplemental life you’ve elected.
Confirm you have long‑term disability coverage; many families treat this as income protection.

6) Other “hidden” or easy‑to‑miss benefits

Action:

Scan your benefits guide for:
EAP (counseling, legal/financial consults)
Tuition reimbursement / learning stipends
Wellness benefits (gym, wellness bonuses)
Legal plans, identity theft protection, pet insurance (if you already spend in those areas)

How to tighten this once June data appears in Monarch

When your June transactions finish syncing, you can:

Pull June spending by category and:
Sum health‑related categories → compare to HSA/FSA options.
Sum childcare/eldercare → compare to Dependent Care FSA limits.
Sum commuting → compare to commuter benefit caps.
Look at paycheck transactions to see which benefits you’re already paying for and at what levels.

If you tell Monarch roughly what you spend in a typical month on (1) health/medical, (2) childcare/eldercare, and (3) commuting, Monarch can turn that into a more specific, numbers‑based checklist for your family.
